#Complex number
Z = 1 + ί

#Modulus of Z
r = abs(Z)

#Angle of Z
theta = arg(Z)

#Number of roots
n = Slider(2, 10, 1, 1, 150, false, true, false, false)

#Plot n-roots
nRoots = Sequence(r^(1/n) * exp( ί * ( theta/n + 2 * pi * k/n ) ), k, 0, n-1)

Roots of Complex Numbers

Recall that if @@math:17:bc585b@@ is a nonzero complex number, then it can be written in polar form as @@math:18:55a7b6@@ where @@math:19:da9fc5@@ and @@math:20:bbd762@@ is the angle, in radians, from the positive @@math:21:9556af@@-axis to the ray connecting the origin to the point @@math:22:c92c68@@

Now, de Moivre's formula establishes that if @@math:23:fd966c@@ and @@math:24:c42d65@@ is a positive integer, then @@math:7:29977a@@

Each value of @@math:34:5a3960@@ gives a different value of @@math:35:97c58e@@ Any other value of @@math:36:6c93ea@@ merely repeats one of the values of @@math:37:ccd10f@@ corresponding to @@math:38:b7773b@@ Thus there are exactly @@math:39:9a18d0@@th roots of a nonzero complex number.

Example: The complex number @@math:44:691435@@ has three cube roots. In this case @@math:45:f794ae@@ and @@math:46:a07fdb@@ Thus the polar of the given number is @@math:47:f13d12@@ Using (\ref{n-roots}) we then obtain

\[ w_k = \sqrt[3]{1}\left[\cos\left(\frac{\pi/2}{3}+\frac{2k\pi}{3}\right)+i\sin\left(\frac{\pi/2}{3}+\frac{2k\pi}{3}\right)\right] \]

The applet below shows a geometrical and numerical representation of the @@math:49:5af066@@th roots of a complex number, up to @@math:50:cbf9dc@@ Drag the red point around to change the value of @@math:51:7f6162@@ or activate the check box Specify values to drag the sliders. You can check the roots of the previous example.

@@verbatim:1:3aad86@@
Code

Enter the following script in GeoGebra to explore it yourself and make your own version. The symbol # indicates comments.

#Complex number
Z = 1 + ί

#Modulus of Z
r = abs(Z)

#Angle of Z
theta = arg(Z)

#Number of roots
n = Slider(2, 10, 1, 1, 150, false, true, false, false)

#Plot n-roots
nRoots = Sequence(r^(1/n) * exp( ί * ( theta/n + 2 * pi * k/n ) ), k, 0, n-1)
